title: Ordering food and drink
subtitle: Café, restaurant, and shop vocabulary in context

vocabulary.exampleMeaning: I would like to order a coffee.

hook.text: Everything you've built — vocabulary, cases, verbs — now gets applied to a real situation: ordering in a café or restaurant. You'll see accusative (I want coffee), genitive (a glass of water), and imperatives (bring the bill) all in the same conversation. This is what grammar looks like in action.

why.text: Café and restaurant interactions are high-frequency in travel and daily life, and they concentrate several grammar patterns in a short space. можно + infinitive (polite request), хочу заказать + accusative (ordering), принесите (imperative), счёт (bill). Getting these right makes you sound genuinely functional in Russian.

cards.title: Polite requests — можно
cards.body: можно + infinitive is a versatile polite request: 'may I...', 'can I have...'. It's impersonal — no subject needed. можно can also be followed directly by a noun in accusative for 'can I have [thing]?'. Extremely common in service situations.

examples.meaning: Could I have some water? (воды = genitive, 'some of')

cards.title: Ordering — хочу заказать + accusative
cards.body: The most direct way to order: Я хочу заказать + what you want (accusative). хотеть conjugation: я хочу, ты хочешь, он/она хочет. What you order goes into the accusative. Alternatively: just name the item in accusative.

examples.meaning: I would like to order a coffee.
Give me tea, please. (дайте = give, formal imperative)
A glass of water, please. (воды = genitive: 'of water')

cards.title: Useful café phrases
cards.body: A practical set of phrases that cover most café interactions. Note: принесите (bring) is a perfective imperative — you want the action completed. счёт (bill) is what you ask for at the end.

examples.meaning: Do you have...? (у вас = at you, formal)

questions.prompt: How do you ask for the menu politely?
questions.explanation: можно + noun in accusative. меню is indeclinable (no change). Можно меню?

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I would like to order a coffee'?
questions.explanation: хочу заказать + accusative. кофе is indeclinable. Я хочу заказать кофе.

questions.prompt: How do you ask for the bill?
questions.explanation: Можно счёт? or Принесите счёт, пожалуйста. Both are correct.

cards.title: Food vocabulary — a core set
cards.body: Basic food items you'll encounter ordering. Note the genders — they matter for adjective agreement and the possessive constructions you know.

cards.title: Prices — сколько стоит?
cards.body: To ask the price: Сколько стоит [item]? (How much does it cost?). стоит = costs (3rd person singular of стоить). The answer gives a number + рублей/рубля/рубль — counting zones apply.

examples.meaning: How much does the coffee cost?

questions.prompt: A glass of water please: ___ воды, пожалуйста.
questions.explanation: стакан = glass (for drinks). стакан воды = glass of water (воды = genitive).

questions.prompt: How do you say 'this is very delicious'?
questions.explanation: вкусно = delicious (invariable predicate). Это очень вкусно.

questions.prompt: Bring the bill please: ___ счёт, пожалуйста.
Do you have tea? У вас ___?
questions.explanation: У вас есть + nominative. У вас есть чай?
Это всё. = That's all. Common phrase when finishing an order.

questions.prompt: How much does the coffee cost?
questions.explanation: сколько стоит + item. кофе is indeclinable.

rules: можно + noun/infinitive = polite request ('can I have', 'may I')
принесите = bring please (formal imperative of принести)
стакан воды = a glass of water (воды = genitive — 'of water')
Сколько стоит? = How much does it cost? (стоит = 3rd sg. of стоить)
summary.nextLesson: Next: asking for directions — getting around with the grammar you know.

questions.prompt: Bring the bill please (formal): ___ счёт, пожалуйста.
I would like to order tea: Я хочу заказать ___.
